text:
Running shoes are as important as endurance when it comes to running a marathon - unless you are two-time Olympic gold medallist Abebe Bikila. The legendary long-distance runner is one of the most unique competitors to ever appear at theOlympic Games.

The Ethiopian's unconventional approach to competition and his journey to the 1960 Rome Olympics were anything but ordinary. Bikila, who died at the age of 41 from a cerebral haemorrhage in 1973, was not initially chosen to compete in the Italian games.

He only got the opportunity because his team-mate Wami Biratu sustained an injury while playing football. Despite these circumstances, he went on to win gold in extraordinary fashion - all without wearing any shoes.

Bikila's athletic career started when he was serving as a bodyguard for the Ethiopian Royal Family. His potential was noticed during his training sessions. He first caught the attention of coaches with his habit of running barefoot.

After his unexpected selection for the Olympics, the initial task was to find him a suitable pair of trainers. This proved to be arduous as they could not find any which were the right size and did not leave him with agonising blisters.

Bikila stuck to what he was comfortable with - his bare, unprotected feet running 26.2 miles across concrete, stones and whoever knows what else. The unusual practice of running barefoot brought extra attention and pressure, but Bikila remained unfazed. His focus was solely on the gold medal and his main competitor, Moroccan Rhadi Ben Abdesselam, whom his coach had warned him about.

Bikila's coach told him to look out for No. 26, but a mix-up with the numbers saw Rhadi wear No.185 instead, causing him to frantically search for the Moroccan when he was by his side for the majority of the race. Interestingly, the race started in the late afternoon and ended at night.



Under the starlit sky, it was Bikila who shone the brightest. In the final 500m, it seemed like a close contest between Rhadi and Bikila, but the Ethiopian found an extra gear and sprinted to the finish line, winning by 25 seconds and setting a new world record.

His victory marked the first time an athlete from sub-Saharan Africa had won an Olympic gold medal, paving the way for a golden era of African long-distance runners. Four years later, Bikila finally found a pair of Puma trainers that were comfortable and didn't cause blisters.

Just weeks before the Tokyo Olympics in 1964, he was diagnosed with acute appendicitis and had to undergo surgery. He managed to build such a commanding lead that he entered the 75,000-capacity stadium alone for the final stretch, crossing the finish line a staggering four minutes and eight seconds ahead of Great Britain's silver medallist, Basil Heatley. To further sum up how super human he was, Bikila even celebrated by performing a calisthenics routine for the adoring crowd.

title: Double Olympic champ Abebe Bikile won gold running marathon barefoot | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Patrick Austen-Hardy
description: Abebe Bikila was not even supposed to be at the 1960 Olympics, but he went on to make history in the most extraordinary fashion.
